metadata.title: Claude, On Average
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one accent; callout ink #161511 on cream; code stays Onda dark glass
metadata.note: COLD OPEN LAW: B00 = ClaudeComposerAsk. SPARK-LINE LAW: inner beats pair the spark with one short serif line; typing is B00-only. OUTRO LAW: outro restates the title. Spine: B00 ASK · B01 NOT-DATABASE (code: same prompt, three draws) · B02 DISTRIBUTION (what it actually is) · B03 NEXT-TOKEN (what it does) · B04 TEMP-ZERO (steers, not specifies) · B05 VARIANCE (the feature) · B06 ONE DRAW (the cost) · B07 VERDICT (artifact: the distribution rules) · B08 title-restate outro.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The same prompt run three times gives plausible non-identical answers because Claude draws from a distribution, not a lookup. It works by next-token prediction, generating each token conditioned on the prompt, system message, and prior output. Temperature zero makes the most probable token win, but a different persona shifts what is most probable even at zero. The variance is the creativity, but when exactness matters you script and run a gate rather than certify one draw.

artifactLines: The same prompt run three times gives plausible non-identical answers because Claude draws from a distribution, not a lookup.
It works by next-token prediction, generating each token conditioned on the prompt, system message, and prior output.
Temperature zero makes the most probable token win, but a different persona shifts what is most probable even at zero.
The variance is the creativity, but when exactness matters you script and run a gate rather than certify one draw.

beats.narration_text: The line frozen is not pinned, that temperature zero steers toward likely without specifying an output, is what I want to see for myself. Take one brief and hold temperature at zero. Can you (1) show me two different personas layered onto that same brief and predict how each shifts the most-probable continuation, (2) explain concretely why that means I cannot cache one answer as the right one, and (3) tell me when I should stop prompting and write a script with a gate instead?

props.command: The line frozen is not pinned, that temperature zero steers toward likely without specifying an output, is what I want to see for myself. Take one brief and hold temperature at zero. Can you (1) show me two different personas layered onto that same brief and predict how each shifts the most-probable continuation, (2) explain concretely why that means I cannot cache one answer as the right one, and (3) tell me when I should stop prompting and write a script with a gate instead?

beats.narration_text: Three runs. Same prompt. Three answers — all plausible, none identical. A database returns one row. Claude returns a draw from a distribution. These are not the same operation.

props.title: same prompt, three draws
props.code:
$ claude -p "write one line about focus"
▸ Focus is the art of saying no.

$ claude -p "write one line about focus"
▸ Attention narrows. That's how you get anywhere.

$ claude -p "write one line about focus"
▸ What you choose not to do is the strategy.

beats.narration_text: The alternative — treating Claude like a lookup — breaks immediately. You ask a question. You get an answer. You run it again. Different answer. You can't cache the right one. There is no right one. There's only the distribution.

What it actually does: next-token prediction. At every step it estimates the most likely continuation of everything before it — the prompt, the system message, everything it has already generated. It's not retrieving a stored answer. It's generating, token by token, conditioned on all of that.

Temperature zero makes the most probable token always win. But probable is conditioned on everything in the context. Add a different persona and you shift what's most probable, even at temperature zero. Steering the distribution is not the same as specifying an output. Frozen is not pinned.

This is also why it's useful. The variance is the creativity. You want different takes on the same brief. You want the unexpected riff. The tails of the distribution are where the interesting answers live. You're not sampling noise. You're sampling possibility.

And this is what it costs you. One draw is not the answer. One draw is a sample. If exactness matters — if two runs must agree — you don't prompt. You script. You run the gate. Never certify a single draw as truth.

props.sparkLine: One draw is not the answer.

beats.narration_text: The distribution rules. Same prompt, different draw — that's the definition. Temperature zero steers toward likely; it doesn't specify an exact answer. Variance is the creativity — certify it with a gate, not with faith. Scripts pin the exact. Skills bias the draw. Gates catch the bad draws. The whole system is consequence of one fact: Claude is a distribution.

artifactLines: You're not querying a database. You're sampling a distribution.
Same prompt, different draw — that's not a bug, that's the definition.
Temperature zero steers toward most likely; it does not specify an output.
Variance is the creativity. Certify draws with gates, not with faith.
Scripts pin the exact. Skills bias the draw. Gates catch the bad draws.
